tldr: Cody (Sourcegraph) focuses on AI code search and in-IDE assistance, while CodeRabbit specializes in AI-driven pull request review. The migration is mostly a workflow shift — from chat-style coding help to automated PR review comments — rather than a data export.
why_switch: You want automated AI review comments directly on every pull request
Your team's bottleneck is review throughput, not in-IDE code generation
You prefer a tool that integrates at the Git host level instead of the IDE
You want a subscription with public per-seat pricing instead of custom quotes
Your reviewers want summarized diffs and walkthroughs on large PRs
what_to_export: List of repositories Cody is connected to
Custom prompts, commands, or context configurations saved in Cody
Any indexed private code or context settings
Saved chats or notable answers worth keeping as documentation
IDE-side configuration (model selection, endpoints) if using BYOK
export_method: Cody is primarily an IDE assistant; there is no large user-data export comparable to a database. Treat the migration as a configuration capture rather than a data dump. Within the Cody admin/console, list the connected repositories, organization members, and any custom commands or prompt templates and copy them into a document. If you use BYOK or self-hosted Sourcegraph, record the model providers and endpoints in use. For chats or generated snippets you want to retain, copy them into your own notes or wiki manually — do not assume a structured export exists. Cancel or downgrade billing only after CodeRabbit has been validated on at least a few repositories.
import_method: CodeRabbit is installed as a GitHub or GitLab app at the organization or repository level. Install the app from the marketplace listing, grant it access to the repositories you exported from Cody, and complete the onboarding to choose plan and seats. Configuration is typically committed as a YAML file at the repo root that controls review behavior, language hints, and path filters; create one per repository or use organization-level defaults. Invite team members via your Git host's organization, then map them to CodeRabbit seats. Open a small test PR to confirm review comments and summaries appear before rolling out to production repos.
gotchas: CodeRabbit reviews PRs; it does not replace in-IDE code generation, so developers may still want a separate IDE assistant
Repositories must be hosted on a supported Git provider for the app to work; self-hosted or unusual providers may not be covered
Per-repo YAML config drift can cause inconsistent review behavior across the org — standardize early
Noisy initial reviews on legacy code are common; tune path filters and severity before wide rollout
Seat counting differs from Cody — verify how active contributors are billed before committing to a plan
If you relied on Cody for codebase-wide search, CodeRabbit will not fill that gap

email.subject: Canceling our Cody subscription in 14 days
email.body:
Hi team,

After evaluating our needs, we are moving our AI-assisted workflow from Cody to CodeRabbit, which fits our pull request review process better. Please treat this as 14 days notice to cancel our Cody subscription. Kindly confirm the effective end date and any final invoice. We appreciate the work your team has put in.

Thanks,
Your customer
